answer:
The following works in the corpus contain explicit prayers (direct supplications addressed to a deity) or invocations (formal calls upon a deity or divine power, often in ritual or narrative contexts). I identified these through corpus-wide searches for terms like "prayer", "pray", "invocation", "invoke", and related phrases, followed by verification via chapter readings and targeted passages. Works with only metaphorical, instructional, or incidental mentions (e.g., ritual procedures without quoted text) are excluded. Philosophical treatises like the French *Traité* volumes contain no such instances.

### Cynewulf, *Andreas*
This Old English poem features multiple explicit prayers to God, including narrative depictions of supplication answered by divine response.[^1][^2]

### Cynewulf, *Elene*
Judas offers a lengthy explicit invocation/prayer to God (the "Lord Savior") on Calvary, calling upon divine power to reveal the True Cross, with direct address and pleas.[^3]

### Cynewulf, *Juliana*
The saint Juliana explicitly prays to God amid torment: "Now, shield of heroes, I will ask you, eternal Almighty..." Divine response follows.[^4]

### Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*
Contains the poem "Bachay Ki Dua" ("A Child's Prayer"), an explicit poetic prayer: "My deepest longing comes to my lips as a prayer: O God, may my life be like a candle's flare." Multiple other poems invoke God in prayerful address.[^5]

### Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*
Poems feature explicit prayerful language, e.g., ritualistic or supplicatory addresses akin to prayer.[^6]

### Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*
Continues with direct invocations, e.g., in "Shikwa", communal prayers and calls to God.[^7]

### Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*
Explicit prayer in ghazal: "A spell of prayer is no match for an arduous quest; O Lord, accept my plea..."[^8]

No other works (e.g., Smṛtis like *Āṅgirasa Smṛti* or *Viṣṇu Smṛti*) contain explicit quoted prayers or invocations; they discuss rituals abstractly (e.g., "perform the twilight prayer" or "having performed the invocation") without direct textual examples.[^9]

[^1]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-bdbda7).
[^2]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, [paragraph](/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-74e29a).
[^3]: Cynewulf, *Elene*, [chapter](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/).
[^4]: Cynewulf, *Juliana*, [chapter](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/).
[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/16-bachay-ki-dua/translation/#p-28b7ba).
[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/03-misaal-e-partav-e-mai-tawaf-e-jaam-karte-hain/translation/#p-f730e9).
[^7]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/translation/#p-e1af7a).
[^8]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, [paragraph](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/68-hareef-e-matlab-e-mushkil-nahin-fusoon-e-niyaaz/translation/#p-018190).
[^9]: E.g., *Āṅgirasa Smṛti*, ch. 1 [paragraph](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-8ae8df); *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, ch. 65 [paragraph](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/65/translation/#p-7e9db8).
